stories.title: A Devotion to Saving Graves and History
stories.summary: Jason Bono, 25, turns headstone cleaning into a small business and history project, restoring weathered graves in East Hampton. Using nontoxic solutions, he respectfully preserves the stories of those who built the town, ensuring their legacy isn't forgotten.
stories.extended_summary: For years, names on weatherworn headstones faded beneath layers of dirt and lichen—until Jason Bono began cleaning them one at a time. The 25-year-old turned his Boy Scout passion into Hampton Headstone Care, using specially made nontoxic solutions to restore graves without harming plant or animal life. His goal is to preserve local history, especially as families move away and ancestors' headstones go uncared for. Even abandoned cemeteries get attention, with the town stepping in. Bono’s work ensures that the history of those who built East Hampton remains visible for generations.
stories.display_reason: hero always uses image mode (summary always visible)
stories.headline_split: A Devotion to Saving Graves
stories.fake_headline: Teenager Starts Library for Forgotten Languages in Her Basement

stories.title: Accessible Tools and Printing with Milwaukee Tool Library and Shaky Hands
stories.summary: The Milwaukee Tool Library lets community members borrow tools and equipment for free, from hand drills to wet saws. Founder Evan Maruszewski believes sharing what you have with a neighbor is a tangible way to improve the world.
stories.extended_summary: At the Milwaukee Tool Library, neighbors can borrow tools free of charge—from power drills to compressors—thanks to founder Evan Maruszewski's belief that access matters more than market ownership. The library also houses Shaky Hands, a printing cooperative. Maruszewski explains that a typical power drill sees only 12 minutes of use in its lifetime; collective ownership makes full use of its utility. The space aims to empower the community by removing barriers to tools and equipment for home and commercial projects.

stories.title: Afrofuturism comes to Garfield through public artwork
stories.summary: Pittsburgh artist Shamir Walters debuts 'Everyday Afrofuturism,' an interactive sculpture in Garfield featuring Octavia Butler, Alice Coltrane, and Herbie Hancock. The kinetic installation invites community engagement and expands ideas about Black creativity and possibility.
stories.extended_summary: Artist Shamir Walters — who calls himself a 'futurist with no Afro' — has unveiled an interactive sculpture in Pittsburgh's Garfield neighborhood. 'Everyday Afrofuturism' features rotating panels of Octavia Butler, Alice Coltrane, and Herbie Hancock, three cultural icons who pushed boundaries of Black creativity. The work grew out of years of Afrofuturism-themed STEAM education with Assemble and BOOM Concepts. Walters, who has Cherokee, Seminole, and Choctaw roots, resists easy categorization, but his art invites everyone to imagine the future.
stories.headline_split: Afrofuturism comes to Garfield

stories.title: After dam removal, freshwater mussel species reintroduced to South Anna River
stories.summary: After 200 years, the alewife floater mussel is back in the South Anna River thanks to dam removal. Biologists planted over 750 mussels, hoping fish like herring will help them repopulate in their historic habitat.
stories.extended_summary: For two centuries, the Ashland Mill Dam blocked fish migration on the South Anna River, causing the alewife floater mussel to go 'functionally extinct' in the area. After the dam was removed in 2024, biologists and volunteers reintroduced 750+ mussels into the river. The mussels rely on host fish like herring for their larvae to attach and grow. With the dam gone, fish can now move freely, giving these freshwater mussels a second chance to thrive in their native waters.
stories.headline_split: After dam removal, freshwater mussel species

stories.title: Ahualoa Family Farms: Growing More Than Macadamia Nuts
stories.summary: Chad Cleveland turned Ahualoa Family Farms into a community-centered macadamia nut business on Hawaiʻi Island. The farm works with over 70 small growers and restored the state’s oldest macadamia processing facility.
stories.extended_summary: Chad Cleveland, a farmer with a degree in agronomy, acquired Ahualoa Family Farms in 2017 and has doubled its size through organic growth. The business now partners with over 70 small growers across Hawaiʻi Island, providing them a market for their macadamia nuts. A key milestone was purchasing and restoring Hawai’i’s oldest macadamia nut processing facility. Cleveland says farming is in his heart, and his company prioritizes community and sustainability over profit, ensuring that the tradition of macadamia farming continues for generations.

stories.title: Alaska’s oldest original lighthouse opens to the public
stories.summary: Alaska’s oldest original lighthouse, Eldred Rock Lighthouse, opens to the public for the first time after a six-year volunteer restoration. Located 55 miles north of Juneau, the 120-year-old landmark now welcomes visitors to learn its history.
stories.extended_summary: After six years of volunteer efforts, the Eldred Rock Lighthouse—Alaska’s oldest original lighthouse—opened to the public for the first time in May 2026. The 120-year-old octagonal structure with its iconic red roof has been a beacon for mariners in Lynn Canal. The Eldred Rock Lighthouse Preservation Association took over from the Coast Guard in 2020 and led the restoration. Now, visitors from Haines and Juneau can explore the lighthouse and learn about its important role in Southeast Alaska's maritime history.
stories.headline_split: Alaska’s oldest original lighthouse

stories.title: Allegheny YMCA reopens with accessibility, public health benefits front and center
stories.summary: The Allegheny YMCA reopens after a $26 million renovation, adding new elevators, ramps, and upgraded facilities. The project also transformed 89 housing units and added childcare and medical services for the North Side community.
stories.extended_summary: After a year and $26 million, the Allegheny YMCA on Pittsburgh's North Side reopens as a more accessible community hub. Major upgrades include two new elevators, an entrance ramp for wheelchair users, and state-of-the-art fitness equipment. But the most impactful changes are less visible: 89 fully renovated SRO housing units for lower-income men, a new childcare facility with 38 slots, and an exam room for medical services. Local leaders hailed the broad coalition that made the project possible, ensuring the Y remains a vital resource for everyone.

stories.title: An Eagle Scout Project that Soars – “Go Home Kits” for Adopted Pets
stories.summary: Eagle Scout Ethan Moskowitz delivers 40 'Go Home Kits' to Last Hope animal shelter. Each kit includes a bed, food, toys, and care instructions for newly adopted pets, inspired by his own rescued Beagle Chip.
stories.extended_summary: When Ethan Moskowitz adopted his Beagle Chip from Last Hope seven years ago, he never imagined it would inspire his Eagle Scout project. As a senior at Wantagh High School, Ethan organized a team to create 40 'Go Home Kits' for newly adopted cats and dogs. Each cardboard carton contains a bed, blanket, Science Diet food, toys, bowls, and a care guide. The project took three months of planning and fundraising. Now, each kit gives a new pet family everything they need to start their lives together — all because of a boy and his rescue dog.

stories.title: Aquatic Robots, Drones and Power Tools: STEM Spans All Grades in Oklahoma School
stories.summary: At Dove School of Discovery in Tulsa, students from kindergarten on up build cars, solder metal, and use power tools in STEM class. Teacher Jacqueline Lanning ensures safety but believes in giving kids hands-on experiences—even if they're scared at first.
stories.extended_summary: At Dove School of Discovery, a Tulsa public charter school, STEM education starts early. Teacher Jacqueline Lanning's curriculum combines art with science and engineering, allowing students as young as third grade to use power drills and soldering irons after safety lessons. Lanning walks scared students through the process, and soon they're excited. The school network, with nine campuses, integrates STEM across all grades. This approach shows that when kids are trusted with real tools, their confidence and curiosity soar.

stories.title: At Rest With Jesus in the City That Never Sleeps
stories.summary: A perpetual adoration chapel in Manhattan has transformed St. Joseph's Church, with Sunday Masses now standing-room-only and record confirmations. Dominican priests say the 24/7 prayer space has brought a spiritual revival in the city that never sleeps.
stories.extended_summary: In the heart of Manhattan, a new perpetual adoration chapel has sparked a spiritual renaissance. Since the Divine Mercy Adoration Chapel opened in mid-2023, St. Joseph's Church has seen Sunday Masses pack the pews, confession lines stretch out the door, and confirmation numbers hit record highs. Dominican Father Boniface Endorf says the chapel brings '24/7 prayer' to a 24/7 city. The transformation even increased young-adult attendance from 25 to 250 weekly. For a city that never sleeps, this sanctuary offers an 'oxygen tank for the soul.'

stories.title: Cancer survivor revives Relay for Life in Park Rapids
stories.summary: Cancer survivor Lora Honer revived the Relay for Life in Park Rapids after it dissolved during the pandemic. The 25th anniversary event features a butterfly release, cornhole, and survivors lap, celebrating community resilience and progress in cancer survival rates.
stories.extended_summary: Lora Honer beat cancer when her survival odds were just 1 in 3—now they're 98.5%. When she moved to Park Rapids, she found the Relay for Life had vanished. So she brought it back. On June 12, the Lakes Area MN Relay for Life returns with a butterfly release, cornhole, and overnight camping, all fueled by a community that refused to let the fight against cancer fade away.

stories.title: Cities Lack Consistent Sidewalk Data. Could Crowdsourced Sidewalk Data Be the Key?
stories.summary: Project Sidewalk lets volunteers map sidewalk accessibility from home using Street View. The crowdsourced data helps cities identify broken ramps and missing crosswalks, making streets safer for people with disabilities—all from your couch.
stories.extended_summary: You can improve sidewalk accessibility without leaving your living room. Project Sidewalk uses Google Street View to let volunteers label curb ramps and broken sidewalks as if they're playing a game. The data trains AI to spot issues automatically, solving a problem most cities ignore: they simply don't know the condition of their own sidewalks.

stories.extended_summary: When Ryan Vail's parents opened a coffee shop, they had a mission bigger than lattes: hire people with Down Syndrome and other disabilities. stories.title: Down the tubes: Vermont volunteers crawl culverts to outsmart the next flood
stories.summary: Vermont's Culvert Crawlers train volunteers to monitor the state's 100,000+ culverts, helping small towns prevent flood damage. Born after the 2023 flood, the project gives communities a hands-on way to build resilience against climate change.
stories.extended_summary: A metal tube under a road can save a town from washing away—if it's clear. After Vermont's 2023 flood, volunteers started the Culvert Crawlers to monitor the state's hidden drainage network. Equipped with training and curiosity, they inspect culverts that small towns often can't keep up with. Organizers say it's a tangible way for anyone to help their community weather the next storm.

stories.title: ELGIN TEEN BEATS THE ODDS IN ‘MIRACLE RECOVERY’
stories.summary: An Elgin teen crushed by an SUV survived against 2-8% odds. Juan Tomelloso Chavez went into traumatic cardiac arrest but walked out of the hospital four days later with no deficits. He reunited with the first responders who saved his life.
stories.extended_summary: A 17-year-old was working on an SUV when the lift collapsed, pinning the vehicle onto his chest. He went into cardiac arrest. But within four days, Juan Tomelloso Chavez was walking out of the hospital without a single deficit—a medical miracle with survival odds of just 2 to 8%. At a reunion ceremony, he embraced the paramedics, firefighters, and dispatchers who refused to give up.

ERNEST, HARDY, and Ella Langley traded hits like 'In Color' and 'Loving Life Again' to raise $445,000 for Second Harvest Food Bank. It's the latest chapter in a tradition that's now topped $1.18 million—proof that country music knows how to show up for its community.

stories.extended_summary: LaShana Gilmore was healthy, college-educated—and her concerns still went unheard. She died after giving birth in 2019. Her best friend turned grief into action, founding A Home From Shana. Now, through the LaborLiveLove program, pregnant women facing unstable housing get a safe place to stay, plus support groups and resources. It's a lifeline for mothers who need a home before their babies arrive.

stories.summary: Forest Hills Stadium will donate 25 cents from every beer and cocktail sold this summer to Queens Community House, supporting youth, adults, and seniors. The program could raise $3,000 per show—enough for 120 families' weekly food pantry bags.

That's the cost of weekly food pantry bags for 120 families—a small pour with a big impact.

stories.title: Foundation Searches for Lost Pets at No Cost
stories.summary: Warbuck’s Drone Services in Salem, Ohio, now searches for lost pets for free through the Compassionate Paws Foundation. Drones cover more ground than foot searches, saving panicked owners from costly recovery fees that can hit $1,500.
stories.extended_summary: When a beloved pet bolts out the door, some recovery companies charge $1,500—even if the animal is never found. Paul Groves and Sarah Oaks saw too many families stuck with that bill and launched the Compassionate Paws Foundation. Their drones sweep woods and neighborhoods at no cost, reuniting people with their pets without adding financial stress to an already painful moment.

stories.extended_summary: You can adopt a dog or cat for free—just bring two box fans. Tyler Animal Services is running a fan drive to help people beat the summer heat, and they'll waive adoption fees (normally $120 for dogs, $80 for cats) with your donation. All adopted pets come spayed, microchipped, and vaccinated. It's a cool deal for both you and someone in need.

stories.extended_summary: Chelsea Funk once rappelled down a nine-story building to raise money for Granted, thinking it only granted wishes for terminally ill kids. stories.extended_summary: Curtis House wanted to correct the record about his great-great-great-grandfather, a Civil War veteran thought to be buried in Thorntown's 'Colored Cemetery.' Research revealed only one of three veterans actually rests there, alongside 30 others—mostly in unmarked, forgotten graves. Now a committee is installing a bronze plaque listing every confirmed name, with a QR code linking to biographies. It's a permanent memorial to the Sugar Creek Community, ensuring their stories aren't erased.

stories.summary: Fall River Health Services in Hot Springs, SD, is investing $2.3 million in a subdivision to create 48 affordable housing units for employees. The hospital lost dozens of workers because they couldn't find homes nearby.
stories.extended_summary: In Hot Springs, the hospital was losing potential hires not because of wages, but because there was simply nowhere to live. stories.title: How solar devices can save lives in an emergency
stories.summary: Upstream Access has given away over 300 solar-powered devices to people with disabilities, helping them keep medicine cold, power equipment, and stay connected during outages. They match each device to the person's needs.
stories.extended_summary: 'Access to power is access to life,' says Erin Taylor of Upstream Access. Her Oregon nonprofit has handed out more than 300 solar lights, batteries, and phone chargers to people with disabilities—many on limited incomes who couldn't afford backup power. Before handing over a device, the team learns each person's specific medical needs and shows them how to use it. When the grid goes down, they're ready.

stories.summary: The Tunica-Biloxi Tribe built a rural health clinic from scratch, now serving 1,700 tribal members and 250+ others with wraparound care. Starting with a part-time diabetes nurse, they opened a full clinic in 2020.
stories.extended_summary: What began as a part-time diabetes nurse on the Tunica-Biloxi reservation grew into a fully fledged clinic offering ambulatory care, behavioral health, and telehealth—built during the pandemic. Today it serves 1,700 tribal members and 250 non-tribal patients, with wraparound services coordinating everything from nutrition to medical referrals. It took a decade of careful planning and partnerships, proving that rural health care can be built from the ground up.

With 157 graduates and a 76% employment rate, the program proves that with the right support, these students can launch real careers, not just entry-level jobs.

stories.title: Librarians on Horseback
stories.summary: In 1935, Kentucky's Pack Horse Library Project sent librarians on horseback to deliver books and handmade scrapbooks to rural residents with no library access. These librarians turned discarded magazines into curated volumes on recipes, quilting, and more—sparking a community exchange of knowledge.
stories.extended_summary: Sixty percent of Kentucky's population had no library access. So the New Deal program created a mobile library on horseback—and when funding fell short, librarians salvaged worn magazines by creating scrapbooks tailored to patron requests. Soon, women were contributing their own recipes and quilting patterns, turning the project into a two-way knowledge swap that transformed isolated communities.

When they roll up sleeves, tiny kidney tattoos appear—not flowers or hearts, but a symbol of the gift that changed everything. Julie had battled kidney disease since seventh grade; Eliana's donation gave her a second chance. The matching ink? A quiet tribute to the life they share.

stories.summary: Lompoc Unified School District approved a strategic arts plan to give every student access to dance, music, visual arts, theater, and media arts by 2030. The vote came after a presentation where arts specialist Jackalynn Snow sang 'Unlimited' from Wicked to rally support.
stories.extended_summary: Jackalynn Snow belted out 'Unlimited' from Wicked at the school board meeting, urging members to 'defy gravity' and approve the plan. The unanimous vote marked a turning point for arts education.

stories.title: Lunch ladies prepared 750 meals for first week of summer meal pickups
stories.summary: Newton High School's lunch ladies packed nearly 750 meal boxes for families in need this summer—each containing five days' worth of breakfasts, lunches, and milk. The program, open to any family in Jasper County, is a lifeline that keeps kids fed when school is out.
stories.extended_summary: Inside the high school kitchen, a small team of lunch ladies moves like an assembly line, grabbing raisins and fruit cups, filling plastic sacks. They've been at it for days, but their energy doesn't flag. Last year, traffic for pickups backed up into the street; this year, they expect even more families. Food Service Supervisor Julie Miller says registrations rise every week—because when school closes, hunger doesn't take a vacation.

stories.title: Mayor Brandon Scott Pushes Back On The Absent Black Father Myth — 1 Stroller Walk At A Time
stories.summary: Baltimore Mayor Brandon Scott launched the city's first Father's Stroller Walk to celebrate Black fatherhood and challenge the myth of absent Black dads. He's backed by data showing Black fathers who live with their children are more involved in daily caregiving than their White or Hispanic counterparts.
stories.extended_summary: Mayor Scott pushes a stroller through Baltimore, side by side with City Councilman Paris Gray, inviting men across the city to do the same. The image counters a stereotype that has persisted for generations. 'Black fathers, despite the rumors, are more involved with their kids than their counterparts,' Scott says. The data backs him up: Black fathers who live with their children lead in daily caregiving activities. For Scott, it's personal—he's a 42-year-old father raising his own kids.
stories.headline_split: Mayor Brandon Scott Pushes Back On The Absent Black Father Myth —

stories.title: NYC Mayor Mamdani Announces Free Knicks Watch Party for NBA Finals Game 3 After MSG's Canceled
stories.summary: When the Knicks' watch party outside Madison Square Garden was canceled due to President Trump's visit, NYC Mayor Zohran Mamdani stepped in to host a free watch party for 5,000 fans at Bryant Park. Fans quickly registered online, and additional venues filled up fast.
stories.extended_summary: Knicks fans thought they'd have to watch Game 3 from home after MSG's watch party was nixed—Secret Service concerns, they said. Then Mayor Mamdani announced a free alternative at Bryant Park, and 5,000 spots vanished within hours. The move turned a disappointment into a citywide celebration, proving that even a presidential visit can't stop New York's love for its team.

stories.title: Northwestern wheelchair basketball pushes for a team
stories.summary: Northwestern University launched a wheelchair basketball team after co-founders Alec Cabacungan and Sam Milgrom turned a one-on-one challenge into inclusive pickup games. The first event drew 15-20 players—way more than the eight they expected—including members of the women's club basketball team.
stories.extended_summary: The sound of basketball shoes was replaced by the hum of wheels at Northwestern's gym. Co-founders hoped for eight players; they got nearly 20, and the energy was electric. The team was born from a 2020 challenge between a student and his adviser—both basketball lovers. Now, players of all abilities compete together, proving that sports can be accessible to everyone.

stories.title: One Wild offseason question: Will the Foligno brothers reunite?
stories.summary: Wild forward Marcus Foligno won the King Clancy Memorial Trophy for his humanitarian work, and his brother Nick surprised him at a hospital visit to present the award. The brothers, who lost their mother to breast cancer, have raised over $200,000 through the Janis Foligno Foundation.
stories.extended_summary: Marcus walked into a University of Minnesota cancer center expecting to meet kids—instead, he found his brother Nick holding the King Clancy Trophy. Nick had flown in from Columbus to pull off the surprise. The award honors players who lead on and off the ice, and the Folignos have done just that: after losing their mother to breast cancer in 2009, they've raised hundreds of thousands for research, turning grief into a legacy of hope.

stories.title: Operation Chill Out: Salvation Army helps homeless people escape heat
stories.summary: As Arizona temps climb, The Salvation Army Tucson is launching Operation Chill Out to help people experiencing homelessness escape the dangerous heat. The initiative combines mobile canteen trucks distributing cold water and supplies with a cooling center that offers showers, meals, and even pet gear—because nobody should face 110-degree days alone.
stories.extended_summary: When it's 110 degrees and someone is sitting on the street with their dog, your heart breaks—that's what drives The Salvation Army Tucson's Operation Chill Out. On days expected to hit 102°, a canteen truck visits city parks with cold water, snacks, hygiene supplies, and heat relief items. Meanwhile, the Hospitality House shelter transforms into a cooling center for up to 35 people daily, offering showers, clean clothes, meals, and a place to rest—plus protective booties and food for pets. The goal? Get as many unhoused individuals inside and safe as possible before summer's worst hits.
stories.headline_split: Operation Chill Out: Salvation Army helps

stories.title: PHOTOS, VIDEO: Party in the warehouse! Here’s how WestSide Baby’s 25th birthday bash went
stories.summary: WestSide Baby celebrated 25 years of helping kids by throwing a birthday bash right in their White Center warehouse—complete with stuffies in party hats and a school bus. Founder Donna Pierce, who started it all as a new mom, shared the incredible impact: over half a million children served.
stories.extended_summary: The warehouse where WestSide Baby stores and sorts supplies for kids became a party space for one night only—car seats held teddy bears in party hats, and guests tossed diaper bundles for fun. But the real highlight was founder Donna Pierce, a former West Seattle mom who started the nonprofit because she couldn't stand that some families couldn't afford diapers. She watched in awe as the crowd celebrated 25 years and more than half a million kids helped. 'This is what you and we together have built,' she said, surrounded by garlands of past invitations and stacks of throwback cupcakes.

stories.title: Parking Lots Get Hot And Are Bad for Storm Runoff. These Groups Are Testing Other Options.
stories.summary: Cities are ditching traditional asphalt for parking lots that actually fight climate change. From porous concrete that absorbs rain to native plant gardens that beat the heat, these new designs stay cooler, prevent flooding, and look way more inviting than blacktop.
stories.extended_summary: In Hampton Roads, Virginia, the crumbling parking lot got a major upgrade—porous concrete panels that let rain sink in faster than it can puddle, plus native landscaping that keeps the surface noticeably cooler. 'You really can tell a difference,' says water resources planner Jill Sunderland. This is just one example of cities rethinking parking lots: New Orleans requires permeable paving, Indianapolis turned lots into rain gardens, and Denver's 'dePaving' initiative aims to slash impervious cover. These aren't just eco-friendly tweaks—they're transforming how urban spaces handle heat and stormwater, making parking lots part of the climate solution.
stories.headline_split: Parking Lots Get Hot And Are Bad for Storm Runoff. These Groups Are Testing
stories.fake_headline: Community Garden Transforms Vacant Lot into Free Produce Market for Low-Income Families

stories.title: Private Graduation Ceremony a True Act of Kindness
stories.summary: When Samantha Klagholz's grandmother, battling stage 4 breast cancer, was given just months to live, her one wish was to see her granddaughter graduate. Southern Regional High School administrators stepped up in a matter of days, organizing a private ceremony so she could witness the milestone she'd fought so hard to see.
stories.extended_summary: Samantha's grandmother, Donna, had clung to one goal through ten and a half years of chemo: making it to June graduation. Then her health took a sudden turn. Assistant Superintendent Megan Vile didn't hesitate when the family reached out—she and the principal and superintendent planned a private graduation in days. Surrounded by family, Samantha walked across a makeshift stage while her Nanny, weakened but beaming, watched from a seat of honor. 'A moment we will cherish forever,' the family wrote. The ceremony was small, but it held a love that filled the room.
stories.headline_split: Private Graduation Ceremony a True Act of

stories.title: Quadrel Tenants Union reach deal with landlord
stories.summary: After more than a year of negotiations, tenants at Quadrel-owned buildings in Evanston won a landmark deal: a 5% rent cut for long-term tenants, capped utility costs, and refunds for overpaid fees. Community organizer Jonah Karsh called it 'a testimony to the work tenants put in.'
stories.extended_summary: When Quadrel Realty Group bought their buildings, tenants faced steep rent hikes, unexplained fees, and maintenance problems. So they unionized. After 12 months of bargaining, they secured a deal covering 120 units: long-term tenants get a 5% rent decrease, two-year lease options, frozen utility costs, and refunds for overpaid fees going back to 2025. 'Really, it's a testimony to the work that these tenants put in,' said organizer Jonah Karsh. For many facing displacement, this agreement means stability—and proof that organizing works.
stories.headline_split: Quadrel Tenants Union reach deal with

stories.title: RAM Clinic serves over 245 patients in Winchester, Tennessee
stories.summary: Remote Area Medical's pop-up clinic in Winchester, Tennessee, delivered over $200,000 in free care to 245 patients in just two days. From dental cleanings to custom eyeglasses, no one was turned away—and some slept in their cars to be first in line.
stories.extended_summary: At Franklin County High School, a pop-up clinic turned a gym into a lifeline. Patients received free dental care, medical exams, eye exams, and glasses made on-site—all for free. With a total value of more than $200,000, the two-day event drew people desperate for care they couldn't otherwise afford. Many arrived Friday night and slept in their cars to guarantee a spot Saturday morning. For those 245 patients, it meant teeth that no longer hurt, vision to see clearly, and a reminder that healthcare doesn't have to come with a price tag.
stories.headline_split: RAM Clinic serves over 245 patients in Winchester, Tennessee –
stories.fake_headline: Chimpanzee at Local Zoo Learns Sign Language, Teaches Visitors New Words

stories.title: Root2Fruit teaches philosophy of ASAP Safe Zone
stories.summary: In Chicago's Austin neighborhood, the Root2Fruit nonprofit helped cut violence by 69% in five years using a simple idea: let young people lead. Now they're teaching other communities the 'Safe Zone' model that turned a park into a symbol of safety and hope.
stories.extended_summary: Kids in Austin identified the spaces that felt safe during the pandemic—places that reminded them of childhood good times. Root2Fruit turned those spots into 'Safe Zones' where youth lead cleanups and build relationships with neighbors. The result? A 69% drop in violence over five years, so impressive that the City Council recognized it as Chicago's first official Safe Zone. Now Root2Fruit is sharing the blueprint: a three-day activation event showed other organizations how to turn young people into active partners in community safety. No more waiting for adults—the next generation is building peace themselves.
stories.headline_split: Root2Fruit teaches philosophy of ASAP Safe Zone –

stories.title: SCFD and SFD recently presented LMHS Junior Lincoln Sharpe with a Citizen’s Award and Honorary Firefighter Badge
stories.summary: Sixteen-year-old Lincoln Sharpe saw a flicker of light from his window, pounded on every door, and got his entire family—plus their dogs—out of their burning home. For his quick thinking, he received a Citizen's Award and an honorary firefighter badge.
stories.extended_summary: It was 11:27 p.m. when Lincoln noticed a flicker outside his window on Long Pond Drive. He looked—and saw flames on the patio. Without hesitation, the 16-year-old ran through the house, pounding on each of the five other bedroom doors and yelling 'Fire!' Every family member scrambled out safely, including the dogs. Fire crews arrived within four minutes, but the home was a total loss. Still, firefighters recovered precious items: a wedding ring, a teddy bear, a military flag, an urn. 'Property can be rebuilt, but saving lives is priority one,' said Battalion Chief Chris Baker. Lincoln's courage earned him a special badge—and the gratitude of eight people alive tonight.
stories.headline_split: SCFD and SFD recently presented LMHS Junior Lincoln Sharpe with a Citizen’s Award and Honorary Firefighter Badge for
fake_endings: saving a family from a fire

stories.title: Shaggy Astoria ‘dispatcher’ leans in for love
stories.summary: The Astoria Police dispatch team has a new furry colleague: Ruthie, a St. Bernard mix who escaped a shelter to become 'dispatch dog.' It's part of a program that's already found 20 dogs their forever homes, one woof at a time.
stories.extended_summary: When Ruthie the St. Bernard mix landed at Clatsop County Animal Shelter, she was one of many. But the Astoria Police dispatch team saw potential—they've been fostering dogs as 'furry dispatchers' for nearly two years, finding 20 dogs homes so far. Ruthie was chosen for her calm demeanor (no barking during 911 calls, please). Firefighters gave her a welcome bath with special shampoo. Now she's hanging out at the dispatch center, leaning in for love and waiting for that special adopter. It's an unlikely partnership: a shelter dog, a police department, and a community that believes every animal deserves a second chance.
stories.headline_split: Shaggy Astoria ‘dispatcher’ leans in for
stories.fake_headline: New Program Gives Free Groceries to Seniors Who Volunteer at Local Schools

stories.title: Sisters forest gets a scrubbing
stories.summary: On May 18, a massive volunteer effort hauled five tons of rubbish from five sites in the Sisters forest. The cleanup, organized by the Care for the Land program, brought together Forest Service staff, firefighters, and community members to restore beloved wild spaces.
stories.extended_summary: Sisters Ranger District, SCLI, and Discover Your Northwest rallied volunteers for a massive cleanup targeting five dump sites. Groups of up to 25 people fanned out across the forest, from easy-access spots behind hotels to remote garbage piles. They hauled away five tons of rubbish—tires, appliances, trash—and capped the day with a barbecue. 'This is just the beginning,' said coordinator Christina Edwards, whose Care for the Land program focuses on stopping illegal dumping and building stewardship habits. For the volunteers, it was a chance to scrub the forest clean and leave it better than they found it.
stories.headline_split: Sisters forest gets a scrubbing –
